title: Brahma-Sūtra refutation of Vaiśeṣika atomism
attacker_text: Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.11–17, with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya as the principal unfolding; supplementary engagements in Rāmānuja's Śrī-Bhāṣya and Vedānta Deśika's Nyāya-Pariśuddhi
target_position_summary: Classical Vaiśeṣika (the Kaṇāda-tradition as developed in Praśastapāda's Padārtha-Dharma-Saṃgraha): the world is composed of paramāṇus (eternal indivisible atoms) of four kinds (earth, water, fire, air); the manifest order arises by combination (saṃyoga) of atoms into dyads, triads, and larger composites; mokṣa is the cessation of qualities of the ātman.

arguments.name: argument-1: BS 2.2.11 — atoms cannot combine without an external principle
premises: Vaiśeṣika holds that paramāṇus exist independently and combine to form composites.
Combination requires either (i) an internal disposition of the atoms toward combination, or (ii) an external principle that brings them together.
If (i): the disposition would be perpetually active (since atoms are eternal), and the world would never be in pralaya — contradicting the cyclical-cosmology Vaiśeṣika accepts.
If (ii): the external principle is itself either an atom (regress) or a non-atom (admits a non-atomic principle, contradicting strict atomism).
arguments.inference: Atoms cannot account for combination on Vaiśeṣika's own resources.
arguments.conclusion: The atomic cosmology requires a non-atomic principle (Brahman or Īśvara) to function; either Vaiśeṣika collapses into theism or fails to explain manifestation.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Vaiśeṣika atomism generally; engaged at the dialectical level, not at the level of any specific Vaiśeṣika thinker.

positions_left_intact.position: Rāmānuja's bhāṣya extends the BS argument with additional considerations on the impossibility of partless atoms acquiring saṃyoga; the Viśiṣṭādvaita extension is largely concordant with Śaṅkara's.

later_responses.response_text: Late Vaiśeṣika and Naiyāyika responses (Udayana's Ātma-Tattva-Viveka) admit Īśvara as the external principle bringing atoms together; this concedes the polemic's force
later_responses.verdict: the historical Vaiśeṣika tradition develops Īśvara-vāda partly in response to such polemics, vindicating the argument's destructive force against strict non-theistic atomism.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive against strict non-theistic Vaiśeṣika. The historical concession (Udayana's Īśvara-Vaiśeṣika) shows the polemic's force; the disagreement does not dissolve but the target school largely concedes the central point.

arguments.name: argument-2: BS 2.2.13 — qualities (guṇas) cannot inhere in partless atoms
premises: Qualities (e.g., colour, taste) are inherent (samavetā) in their substrate.
Inherence presupposes a substrate with parts in which the quality can locate.
A partless atom cannot serve as such a substrate without contradicting partlessness.
Hence the qualities Vaiśeṣika ascribes to atoms have no coherent ontological location.
arguments.conclusion: Vaiśeṣika's substance-quality scheme breaks down at the atomic level.

later_responses.response_text: Later Vaiśeṣika argues that inherence is sui generis (samavāya as a distinct padārtha) and does not require part-whole structure; partless substrates can bear qualities by direct samavāya
later_responses.verdict: the reply requires accepting samavāya as a primitive non-mereological relation; the disagreement reduces to whether such a primitive is dialectically legitimate.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive on a specific ontological question (mereology of substrates). The disagreement does not dissolve.

arguments.name: argument-3: BS 2.2.17 — atomic combination cannot account for emergent qualities
premises: Compounds bear qualities not present in their atomic components (e.g., a pot has bulk, shape, and use that single atoms do not).

Emergent qualities require either (i) the qualities to arise ex nihilo (against Vaiśeṣika's satkārya-asatkārya-vāda hybrid), or (ii) the qualities to derive from the compound's structure rather than its atomic components.
If (i): the emergence is unaccounted for and violates causation principles Vaiśeṣika accepts.
If (ii): structure is a non-atomic feature, again admitting a non-atomic principle.
arguments.inference: Emergent qualities cannot be accounted for atomistically.
arguments.conclusion: The world's manifest properties cannot be reduced to atomic substrates and their saṃyogas; a non-atomic ground is required.

later_responses.response_text: Later Vaiśeṣika admits a doctrine of viśeṣa-guṇa-utpatti (emergence of compound-specific qualities) but does not satisfactorily ground it on strictly atomic terms
later_responses.verdict: the doctrinal patch addresses the symptom but not the underlying mereological problem.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive on the question of emergent properties — a question that recurs in modern philosophy of mind and physics. Genuine philosophical disagreement; no register-clarification dissolves it.

user_thesis_summary: The anti-Vaiśeṣika polemic is methodologically destructive: it shows that strict atomism cannot account for combination, quality-bearing, or emergence on its own resources. The historical Vaiśeṣika tradition responded by admitting Īśvara as the external organising principle (Udayana) and by treating samavāya as a primitive non-mereological relation. These concessions vindicate the polemic's force without producing a perspectivist resolution. As with the anti-Sāṃkhya polemic, all major Vedāntic schools agree on the substantive conclusion (Vaiśeṣika atomism alone is insufficient), so the polemic is one of inter-Vedāntic alignment rather than inter-Vedāntic disagreement. The polemic does not bear directly on the perspectivism hypothesis among Vedāntic schools, but it illustrates the BS bhāṣya tradition's methodological commitment to refuting non-Vedāntic darśanas where they conflict with the śruti-attested account. Verdict: substantive; the target school historically concedes the central point. Not relevant to inter-Vedāntic perspectivism.
primary_source_loci: Vedānta Deśika, Nyāya-Pariśuddhi (the constructive Viśiṣṭādvaita engagement with Naiyāyika and Vaiśeṣika ontology)

scholarly_apparatus.work: On Being and What There Is: Classical Vaiśeṣika and the History of Indian Ontology (SUNY, 1992)
scholarly_apparatus.position: Documents the historical evolution of Vaiśeṣika in response to its critics, including the BS polemic; reads Udayana's Ātma-Tattva-Viveka as the constructive Vaiśeṣika-Nyāya response that admits theism.

scholarly_apparatus.work: Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, vol. 2 (Indian Metaphysics and Epistemology)
scholarly_apparatus.position: Standard reference for the Vaiśeṣika doctrines engaged by the BS polemic and the historical Vaiśeṣika responses.
